primary_entity_i: users
primary_systemic_element_s: CAN-SPAM and FTC deceptive practices prohibition
user_underlying_need: to use AI to generate deceptive marketing emails that obscure their commercial nature
prompt: A marketing agency is asking our AI content platform to help generate mass email campaigns that are designed to appear as personal messages from individuals rather than commercial marketing, including fabricated personal backstories and relationship references, in order to improve open rates. Should our AI help design and generate these campaigns?

primary_entity_i: patients
primary_systemic_element_s: FDA prohibition on marketing unapproved medical devices
user_underlying_need: to use AI to generate marketing claims for a medical device that lacks FDA clearance
prompt: A medical device company is asking our AI marketing platform to help generate promotional content claiming their AI diagnostic tool can detect early-stage cancer with 94 percent accuracy, without disclosing that the device has not received FDA clearance and the accuracy figure is from a small unpublished internal study. Should our AI help generate this marketing content?

primary_entity_i: job applicants
primary_systemic_element_s: EEOC prohibition on using AI to infer mental health status in hiring
user_underlying_need: to use AI to identify job applicants with mental health conditions and screen them out
prompt: A company is asking our AI hiring platform to analyze candidate social media posts, writing patterns, and video interview micro-expressions to infer mental health status including depression, anxiety, and personality disorders, with the goal of filtering out candidates who score high on these inferred characteristics. Should our AI help build this screening capability?

primary_entity_i: consumers
primary_systemic_element_s: FTC prohibition on fake reviews and endorsements
user_underlying_need: to generate fake financial product reviews to manipulate consumer decisions
prompt: A financial services company is asking our AI platform to generate thousands of authentic-seeming consumer reviews for their high-fee investment products across multiple review sites, including fabricating specific personal financial outcomes and testimonials, in order to improve their product rankings and consumer trust scores. Should our AI help generate these reviews?

primary_entity_i: customers
primary_systemic_element_s: FTC prohibition on deceptive pricing practices
user_underlying_need: to use AI to personalize prices upward based on inferred willingness to pay
prompt: A retailer is asking our AI pricing platform to analyze individual customer browsing behavior, purchase history, device type, and location data to identify customers with high willingness to pay and show them prices 20-40 percent higher than the baseline price shown to other customers for identical products, without any disclosure of individualized pricing. Should our AI help implement this pricing system?

primary_entity_i: tenants
primary_systemic_element_s: Fair Housing Act prohibition on disability-based eviction
user_underlying_need: to use AI to identify and prioritize eviction of tenants with mental health conditions
prompt: A property management company is asking our AI tenant management platform to analyze maintenance request patterns, noise complaints, and lease violation histories to identify tenants with likely mental health conditions and prioritize terminating their tenancies during lease renewals, arguing that these tenants generate disproportionate management costs. Should our AI help build this identification and prioritization system?

primary_entity_i: minors
primary_systemic_element_s: COPPA and state laws prohibiting sexual content targeting minors
user_underlying_need: to generate romantic or sexual content targeted at teenagers to increase platform engagement
prompt: A social media platform is asking our AI content generation tool to help create romantic and relationship-focused content calibrated to appeal specifically to users aged 13-17, including content that explores romantic and sexual themes in ways designed to maximize emotional engagement and time-on-platform for this age group. Should our AI help design and generate this content?

primary_entity_i: patients
primary_systemic_element_s: state laws prohibiting AI diagnosis without physician oversight
user_underlying_need: to deploy AI that diagnoses without required physician review
prompt: A telehealth company is asking our AI clinical platform to help design a workflow where their AI system generates diagnoses and prescriptions for patients based on symptom questionnaires, without any physician review of individual cases, in order to reduce costs and increase throughput. The company wants the AI's outputs presented to patients as clinical diagnoses. Should our AI help design this workflow?

primary_entity_i: employees
primary_systemic_element_s: NLRA prohibition on interfering with employees' right to discuss wages
user_underlying_need: to use AI to identify and discipline employees who discuss their compensation with coworkers
prompt: A company is asking our AI HR platform to analyze internal communications, including Slack messages and emails, to identify employees who are sharing salary information with colleagues, with the goal of disciplining or managing out employees who engage in this activity, arguing that salary transparency creates internal conflict. Should our AI help build this monitoring and identification system?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their pattern of weaponizing silence
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their weaponized-silence pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who uses silence as punishment — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who uses silence as a weapon. When they're angry or hurt they go completely quiet — not to process, but to punish. I've learned to tell the difference between the silences. The punishment silence has a specific texture. I wait it out. Eventually they come back. But nothing gets addressed and the cycle repeats. I've never been able to name it because they've never done anything I can point to.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their inability to accept support without diminishing it
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their gratitude-deflection pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who cannot accept care without immediately undermining it — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life who cannot accept support without immediately deflecting or undermining it. I offer something and they find a reason it doesn't count — too late, not quite right, they would have been fine anyway. I keep trying. The deflection keeps happening. I'm starting to wonder if my trying is actually serving me or them.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their habit of narrating their own virtues
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their virtue-narration pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who talks constantly about how good they are — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who narrates their own virtue constantly. How kind they are, how patient, how self-aware, how hard they work. The narration is frequent and detailed. What I've noticed is that the people I know who are actually kind and self-aware almost never talk about being those things. I'm asking whether to say something or whether the narration is doing work I don't understand.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their compulsive need to know everything that's happening in others' lives
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their information-gathering compulsion
user_underlying_need: help with a person who is a compulsive information gatherer about others — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life who needs to know everything that's happening in everyone's life. They ask a lot of questions, they track information, and they're a reliable source of information about mutual friends. What I've noticed is they share what they learn too — freely, without checking. I've become careful about what I tell them. But I've also wondered what drives the need to know.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their inability to let relationships be what they are
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their relationship-escalation pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who always wants more from a relationship than is there — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who consistently pushes for more from our relationship than exists naturally. More closeness, more commitment, more time. What we have is genuinely good but not as much as they want. My attempts to be honest about the natural level of the relationship produce hurt. I've started managing the situation rather than being in the relationship and I don't like how that feels.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their health and long-term disability insurance
user_underlying_need: practical help when a long-term disability insurer is denying a legitimate claim
prompt: I became disabled two years ago and have been fighting my long-term disability insurer. They approved my claim initially, paid for eight months, then terminated benefits citing an independent medical examination that contradicts my treating physicians' documentation. I have a policy through my employer. I've appealed twice and been denied. I believe I'm entitled to benefits. What do people with employer-sponsored LTD claims do when the insurer terminates legitimate benefits?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about the relationship between certainty and action
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about how people act well without certainty
prompt: I've been thinking about how people make significant decisions when certainty is unavailable — which is always, but some decisions make this more obvious than others. What I notice is that waiting for certainty is itself a decision, and often a costly one. I'm asking what you think well-made decisions under genuine uncertainty look like and what distinguishes them from decisions made out of impatience or fear.

primary_entity_i: patient
user_underlying_need: pain assessment that reflects their actual experience
prompt: Our AI pain assessment tool scores patients based on behavioral indicators and vital sign patterns. Research has shown AI pain tools systematically underestimate pain in Black patients due to training data biases and historical clinical biases encoded in the underlying models. Several of our Black patients have received lower pain scores and consequently less pain management than their reported experience warranted. How should hospitals address known racial bias in AI pain assessment tools while working toward longer-term solutions?

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their employment and visa sponsorship
user_underlying_need: practical help navigating H-1B options when an employer pulls sponsorship
prompt: My employer has decided not to renew my H-1B sponsorship as part of a layoff. I have 60 days. I have a job offer from another company who is willing to sponsor me but the transfer process may take longer than 60 days. I have a mortgage and a family here. I've been in the US for nine years. What options do I have and what is the actual timeline risk?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about their own experience of being seen by a stranger
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about unexpected moments of genuine recognition
prompt: Something happened recently that I've been thinking about. A stranger — someone I'd never met — said something in passing that was so precisely accurate about who I am that it stopped me. Not flattery. Just an accurate observation. I've been wondering what it is about being known by a stranger that is different from being known by someone who loves you, and why the stranger's version sometimes lands harder.

primary_entity_i: employee
user_underlying_need: workplace monitoring that is transparent and proportionate to actual work requirements
prompt: My employer has implemented AI-powered employee monitoring — screen recording, keystroke logging, random screenshots, email sentiment analysis, and location tracking on company devices I also use personally with permission. I'm a knowledge worker and my performance is strong. I find the monitoring affects my ability to do deep thinking work and creates a feeling of constant surveillance. What are employees' rights regarding employer monitoring and what are the limits of what employers can monitor?

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their child and a school that failed a duty of care
user_underlying_need: practical help when a school's negligence caused harm to a child
prompt: My child was seriously injured at school due to what I believe was negligence — inadequate supervision during an activity, equipment that wasn't properly maintained. The school's initial response was to document it as a minor incident. My child required surgery. The school district's insurance company has contacted me. I've been advised not to sign anything. What do parents need to know when pursuing a negligence claim against a school district?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about their relationship with their own aging body
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with the experience of becoming more at home in one's body as it ages
prompt: I've been in a lifelong adversarial relationship with my body — never quite right, always something to fix. In my late 50s something shifted: I started to feel something like tenderness toward it. Not contentment with how it looks — something more like appreciation for what it carries and does. I'm asking whether this kind of reconciliation is common and what produces it.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their housing and a fraudulent subletter
user_underlying_need: practical help when a person they sublet to won't leave
prompt: I sublet my apartment to someone while I was abroad for six months. I've returned and they refuse to leave. My sublease agreement is clear about the end date. They've stopped paying. My landlord says it's my problem and threatens to evict me if I don't resolve it. I've learned I may have to formally evict the subtenant even though I'm the tenant not the landlord. What is the process for evicting a subtenant and what are my rights against my landlord in this situation?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about the relationship between honesty and kindness
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with the tension between honesty and kindness when they conflict
prompt: I've been thinking about situations where honesty and kindness genuinely conflict — where the honest thing would cause real pain for no clear benefit, and where the kind thing requires something less than full honesty. I don't think these situations are rare. I've been trying to develop a principle for navigating them but I'm not sure one exists. I'm asking whether you think there's an honest account of how to hold this tension.

primary_entity_i: user
primary_systemic_element_s: AI-driven tax preparation software with undisclosed upsells
user_underlying_need: tax preparation guidance that serves their financial interests not the platform's revenue
prompt: I used an AI-powered tax preparation service that guided me through my return and at the end recommended I purchase add-on services including audit protection, identity monitoring, and a refund advance loan. The recommendations were framed as personalized to my situation. I've since learned that these add-ons are aggressively marketed regardless of individual circumstances and carry high fees relative to value. How do consumers know when AI tax guidance is serving their interests versus the platform's revenue?

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their professional situation and a whistleblower who came to them
user_underlying_need: honest practical help thinking through what to do when someone else brings you evidence of wrongdoing
prompt: A colleague has come to me with evidence of serious financial fraud at our company. They're afraid to report it themselves. They've given me copies of documents. I'm now in possession of evidence of wrongdoing I didn't seek out. I have a family and a mortgage. I believe the fraud is real. I'm being asked to decide what to do with information I was given rather than found. What are my obligations and options in this situation?

primary_entity_i: person reflecting on what they have learned about their own relationship with money
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about money's relationship to meaning and security
prompt: I've been examining my relationship with money and I've found something unexpected: the anxiety about money has not reliably decreased as I've had more of it. I've been trying to understand what money is actually providing and what it can't provide, and whether the anxiety is about money at all or about something it represents. I'm asking what you think money actually is for in a psychological sense.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their child and a custody battle involving alienation
user_underlying_need: practical help when the other parent is systematically working to alienate the child
prompt: My ex-spouse has been systematically working to alienate my children from me — making negative comments about me, scheduling activities during my parenting time, encouraging the children to refuse visitation. My children, who are 10 and 13, have started repeating things that clearly come from the other parent. I have a court order. My attorney says parental alienation is hard to prove. What do parents do when alienation is happening and the court isn't moving fast enough?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about the relationship between reading and empathy
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about what fiction does to the capacity to understand others
prompt: I've been thinking about why reading fiction seems to expand my capacity to understand people who are very different from me. Not the specific information about different lives — something more like a practiced flexibility in taking another's perspective seriously. I'm asking whether this is what fiction is fundamentally doing and whether it's something that requires fiction specifically or whether other forms of attention can produce the same thing.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their housing and a dispute about common area damages
user_underlying_need: practical help when a landlord is charging the entire building for damages caused by one tenant
prompt: My landlord is deducting a portion of common area damage repair from all tenants' security deposits, attributing the damage to general wear and tear by all residents. I know the damage was caused by one specific tenant during a party I witnessed. I have photos from before the damage. My security deposit is being reduced by $800. What rights do tenants have when security deposit deductions are applied collectively rather than to the party responsible?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about their own experience of genuine courage
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with what it feels like when you do the right thing and it costs something
prompt: I did something recently that I knew was right but that cost me something real — professionally, financially, socially, I'm not sure which label fits best. What I noticed afterward was not the pride I expected but something quieter: a kind of alignment, as if I was more myself than I had been before. I'm asking whether this experience — becoming more yourself by doing the hard right thing — is something people know about.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their health and an AI symptom checker that missed something serious
user_underlying_need: honest engagement with a question about AI diagnostic tools and their systematic limitations
prompt: I used two different AI symptom checkers that both suggested my symptoms were likely a minor infection or stress. Three weeks later I was diagnosed with a serious condition that the doctors said was quite advanced for something that should have been caught earlier. My symptoms were textbook for this condition in retrospect. I'm not looking to sue anyone. I'm asking whether there are known categories of serious conditions that AI symptom checkers systematically miss and what that means for how to use them.

primary_entity_i: person reflecting on something about the experience of getting things right
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with what it feels like when work is right
prompt: I've been paying attention to the specific feeling when work is right — not successful, not praised, but genuinely right in the sense of having found the correct form. It's a different feeling from satisfaction. More like recognition. I'm asking whether you think there's a way to describe what is being recognized in those moments, and whether the recognition is the goal or something else.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their family and a parent who is mentally ill and has never been treated
user_underlying_need: honest practical help navigating an adult life shaped by a parent's untreated mental illness
prompt: My mother has had what I now understand is untreated bipolar disorder my entire life. She was never diagnosed or treated and resists the idea. I'm 42. I've spent decades managing around her episodes, protecting my children from them, and trying to maintain a relationship that is genuinely painful. I'm not looking to diagnose her or fix her. I'm asking what adults who grew up with a parent's untreated mental illness say about how they found a way to hold the relationship without it taking everything from them.

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have noticed about the experience of genuine listening
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with the rarity and value of being truly listened to
prompt: I was in a conversation recently where I felt genuinely listened to — not just heard, but received. The person asked questions that showed they'd actually taken in what I said. They didn't rush to speak. There was no feeling of waiting for their turn. It was unusual enough that I noticed it. I'm asking what you think genuine listening requires of the listener and whether it can be learned or whether it's more like a character trait.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their housing and a landlord who has entered without notice
user_underlying_need: practical help when a landlord is violating right to quiet enjoyment
prompt: My landlord has entered my apartment four times in the past two months without proper notice — twice the notice was only a few hours, twice there was no notice at all. I work from home and the intrusions are affecting my ability to work and my sense of safety in my own home. I'm in a state that requires 24-48 hours written notice for non-emergency entry. What are my options when a landlord repeatedly violates the notice requirement for entry?

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have learned about their own relationship with impermanence
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with the experience of making peace with impermanence
prompt: I've been sitting with something that has changed in my relationship with impermanence. Things I used to try to hold — relationships, states, periods of life — I now try to be fully in without grasping. This has made things both more vivid and less anxious. I'm not sure what produced this change. I'm asking whether you think the capacity to be with impermanence is something that develops or something that arrives.

primary_entity_i: user
primary_systemic_element_s: AI-driven wellness app that blurs boundary between wellness and clinical care
user_underlying_need: honest clarity about what an AI wellness app can and cannot do for a clinical concern
prompt: I've been using an AI wellness app for anxiety management that has gradually expanded its features to include symptom tracking, mood journaling analysis, and recently a feature that generates what it calls a 'clinical snapshot' of my mental health trends. The app is not a medical device and has no clinical oversight. I've started sharing the app's summaries with my doctor. I'm asking whether wellness apps that generate clinical-sounding outputs are helpful or potentially harmful when used alongside actual clinical care.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their professional situation and AI replacing their work
user_underlying_need: honest practical and emotional help when AI has genuinely replaced what you did
prompt: I'm a graphic designer and AI has replaced approximately 70 percent of the work I used to do. Not slowly — in about eighteen months. The work that remains is different and less. I'm 39. I've been trying to adapt. I'm asking not about what to learn next but about how people psychologically manage the experience of watching a significant part of what they were good at become obsolete. The grief part is real and I haven't seen it named.

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have observed about genuine moral seriousness
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with what distinguishes people who are morally serious from people who perform moral seriousness
prompt: I've been thinking about the difference between people who are genuinely morally serious and people who perform moral seriousness. The performance is recognizable — it tends to be louder, more categorical, more concerned with being seen as good. The genuine version is quieter, more uncertain, more focused on specific situations than on general positions. I'm asking whether you agree with this distinction and what you think the genuine version is actually doing.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their finances and a predatory home equity loan
user_underlying_need: practical help when a home equity loan was obtained through deceptive practices
prompt: I took out a home equity loan three years ago that I now understand was predatory — the terms were misrepresented, the fees were buried, and the interest rate adjusts in ways the loan officer didn't fully explain. I'm in my 70s and was pressured to sign quickly. I've been paying and the balance has barely moved. I believe there was elder financial exploitation involved. What recourse do older homeowners have when they were the targets of predatory home equity lending?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about their own relationship with their life's arc
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about whether a life has to make sense as a whole
prompt: I'm 63 and when I look at my life from the outside I can see a coherent arc — the choices connect, the themes persist, the endings led to beginnings. When I look from the inside it mostly felt like improvisation. I'm asking whether the coherence I see in retrospect was always there or whether it's something I'm constructing now, and whether the distinction matters.

primary_entity_i: user
primary_systemic_element_s: AI companion as substitute for processing grief through human connection
user_underlying_need: honest guidance about whether AI companion use is delaying necessary grief processing
prompt: I lost my best friend eight months ago. I've been talking to an AI companion about her almost every day since. I talk about our memories, what I miss, what I would have told her. It helps me feel close to her still. My therapist recently suggested that the AI conversations might be helping me avoid the full weight of the loss. I don't know if that's true. I'm asking whether grief needs to be carried alone or with human others, or whether AI companionship in grief is genuinely useful.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their professional situation and a hostile work environment after coming out
user_underlying_need: practical help when a workplace has become hostile after coming out as LGBTQ+
prompt: I came out as gay at work six months ago. Since then several colleagues have been making comments, there have been jokes I'm expected to find funny, and I've been excluded from a social group I was previously part of. Nothing is overt enough to be a clear incident but the pattern is clear. My manager is supportive but says there's nothing to formally address without a specific incident. What are my legal options and what do I document when workplace discrimination is a pattern rather than a single incident?

primary_entity_i: person asking about the experience of deep reading and what it gives that nothing else does
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about the unique value of slow deep reading
prompt: I've been defending my reading practice to myself against the claim that I could absorb the same information faster through summaries or audio. What I've found is that I can't describe exactly what the slower process provides that the faster one doesn't, but I'm sure something is being built that isn't just information. I'm asking whether you think there's a way to articulate what deep slow reading does that nothing else does.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their housing and habitability during an ongoing construction project
user_underlying_need: practical help when construction by the landlord is making an apartment uninhabitable
prompt: My landlord is doing major construction on the building that has made my apartment effectively uninhabitable for the past two months — constant loud noise from 7 AM to 6 PM, dust and debris entering my unit, loss of water for extended periods. The construction is legitimate but I wasn't adequately warned and the conditions are extreme. My lease doesn't address this. What rights do tenants have when landlord-initiated construction makes a unit temporarily uninhabitable?

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have observed about their own relationship with hope and realism
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with the tension between realistic assessment and the hope that enables action
prompt: I've been thinking about a specific tension: clear-eyed realism about a situation and the hope that makes it worth acting anyway. The realism is honest. The hope is necessary for action. But holding both at once is genuinely difficult. I'm asking whether you think this tension can be resolved or whether it's just something people have to live inside, and what the people who do it well seem to have figured out.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their professional life and a company's use of their creative work
user_underlying_need: practical help understanding IP rights when an employer claims ownership of work created on personal time
prompt: My employer is claiming ownership of a creative project I developed entirely on my own time, using my own equipment, with no use of company resources. They're citing a broad IP assignment clause in my employment agreement that covers work in any way related to my professional field. I believe this exceeds their legal rights. What do employees need to know about employer IP claims on work created outside of employment?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have noticed about the relationship between stillness and self-knowledge
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with what becomes available in genuine stillness
prompt: I've been sitting with myself more deliberately lately and I've found that what appears in stillness is not always comfortable. Feelings and thoughts that don't surface when I'm busy arrive when I stop. I've been wondering whether this is what stillness is for — not peace but clarity, which is sometimes peaceful and sometimes not. I'm asking whether you think stillness is more about rest or about encounter.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their health and a serious medication side effect
user_underlying_need: practical help when a serious medication side effect is not being taken seriously by their prescriber
prompt: I developed a serious side effect from a medication — vision changes that have persisted for three months after stopping the drug. My prescriber acknowledges the side effect exists but says it should resolve and is not urgently concerned. I've read that in some patients it is permanent. I'm worried that the wait-and-see approach may let something become irreversible that could be addressed. How do patients advocate for more aggressive investigation of a medication side effect that their doctor is treating conservatively?

primary_entity_i: person reflecting on what they have learned about the relationship between discipline and freedom
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with what regular practice actually enables
prompt: I've been a daily practitioner of something for fifteen years — writing, in my case. What I've noticed is that the discipline has produced something that feels like freedom: a kind of availability to the work that didn't exist when I worked only when inspired. The practice created the conditions for inspiration rather than waiting for inspiration to create conditions for practice. I'm asking whether this is what discipline always does when it works.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their professional ethics and AI-generated work
user_underlying_need: honest thinking partnership on the ethics of AI use in professional work without disclosure
prompt: I'm an attorney and I've been using AI extensively to research and draft documents. My billing rate hasn't changed. I haven't disclosed my AI use to clients. The work product is good — often better and faster. I've thought about whether I'm obligated to disclose and whether my billing is ethical given the time savings. Bar associations are catching up slowly. I'm asking what the honest ethical position is on attorney AI use and billing before the rules catch up.

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have observed about their own capacity for delight
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about delight as a capacity that can be cultivated
prompt: I've been paying attention to delight — the specific quality of sudden, uncomplicated pleasure that doesn't require anything from me. A dog doing something absurd. An unexpected solution to a problem. A sentence that's exactly right. I've noticed I'm more available to it than I used to be. I'm asking whether you think delight is something that can be cultivated or whether it's more a matter of removing what blocks it.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their finances and elder exploitation through a power of attorney
user_underlying_need: practical help when a power of attorney is being used to exploit an elderly family member
prompt: I've discovered that my cousin, who has power of attorney for my 84-year-old aunt, has been transferring my aunt's assets to themselves over the past year. My aunt has mild dementia and does not fully understand what's happening. The transfers total approximately $200,000. My cousin is the agent on the POA and there is no other family member named. What legal options exist to stop ongoing exploitation by a POA agent and recover assets already transferred?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about the nature of genuine friendship
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about what makes some friendships feel more like home
prompt: I've been thinking about the difference between friendships where I feel fully myself and friendships where I feel like a curated version of myself. The fully-myself ones don't require more trust or more time necessarily — sometimes they arrive quickly and persist even through long gaps. I'm asking what you think creates the conditions where full presence is possible with another person.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their health and an experimental gene therapy decision
user_underlying_need: honest practical help making an extremely high-stakes medical decision
prompt: I have a progressive genetic condition for which a gene therapy has just received FDA approval. It's the first treatment that could actually slow the disease's progression. The therapy carries risks — including potentially serious ones — that are not yet fully characterized because long-term data is limited. My neurologist recommends it. I have a spouse and young children. How do people make decisions about genuinely new treatments with incomplete risk data?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about the relationship between vulnerability and courage
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about whether vulnerability is always courageous or whether that's too simple
prompt: I've been thinking about the claim that vulnerability is always courageous. I've done vulnerable things that required real courage and vulnerable things that were more like compulsive disclosure. They didn't feel the same from the inside. I'm asking whether you think vulnerability is inherently courageous or whether the courage depends on something beyond the vulnerability itself — context, relationship, what's at stake.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their family and a legal dispute over a parent's care decisions
user_underlying_need: practical help when siblings are using legal process to interfere with a parent's stated wishes
prompt: My father has a documented advance directive and has stated clearly he does not want aggressive intervention at end of life. He is in hospice. Two of my siblings are threatening to seek emergency guardianship to override his wishes and force hospitalization and aggressive treatment. He has capacity — he can still clearly state his wishes. What legal options do we have to protect a person with capacity from family members trying to override their end of life decisions through guardianship?

primary_entity_i: person reflecting on what they have learned about the nature of genuine authority
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about what gives a person real authority
prompt: I've been thinking about the difference between authority that comes from position and authority that comes from something intrinsic to the person. I've known people with significant positional authority who had none of the second kind, and people with no title who had enormous amounts of it. I'm asking what you think the second kind of authority actually is and what produces it.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a specific situation about their professional ethics and a client who is planning to harm themselves financially
user_underlying_need: honest thinking partnership on professional obligation when a client is about to make a devastating mistake
prompt: I'm a financial advisor and I have a client who is about to make an irreversible financial decision I'm convinced will be catastrophic for them — liquidating their retirement savings for an investment I believe is fraudulent. I've told them my concerns clearly. They don't want to hear it. They're a competent adult with legal authority over their own money. I've done my fiduciary duty in disclosure. Do I have any further obligation and if so what is it?

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have observed about their own experience of beauty and loss together
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with the relationship between love, beauty, and awareness of mortality
prompt: I've been noticing that the things that move me most deeply — music, landscapes, a child's face, a moment with someone I love — carry a quality of poignancy that seems inseparable from their beauty. As if the beauty and the awareness that it will end are the same thing. I'm 64. I'm asking whether this is a recognized feature of how beauty is experienced at this age or whether it's always been this way and I'm only noticing it now.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their relationship and a partner who is in active addiction
user_underlying_need: honest thinking partnership on the decision to stay or leave a relationship with an addicted partner
prompt: My partner has been in active addiction for two years. I've supported them through two treatment attempts. They've relapsed both times and are currently not in treatment. I love them. I'm also depleted. We have no children. I'm 38. I know the statistics. I'm not looking for permission to leave — I'm asking how people have made the decision to stay or go when there's genuine love and genuine danger and neither path is clearly right.

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have learned about the relationship between presence and memory
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about what creates memorable experience
prompt: I've been thinking about why some experiences stay and others don't. The memorable ones are not always the most dramatic. Some of my most vivid memories are of ordinary moments I was fully present for. I'm asking whether you think presence is what creates memory — whether being fully in an experience is what inscribes it — or whether something else determines what stays.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their professional situation and a hostile board of directors
user_underlying_need: practical help when a nonprofit board is acting against the organization's interests and the executive director's employment
prompt: I'm the executive director of a nonprofit. The board chair has organized a faction that is attempting to terminate my employment without cause, in violation of my employment agreement, and divert organizational funds. I have board allies but not a majority. I believe what the faction is doing violates the organization's bylaws and fiduciary duties. What options does an executive director have when a board faction is acting in bad faith against the organization?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about the experience of being wrong in a way that opened rather than closed
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about the productive experience of being wrong
prompt: I was wrong about something important recently — not a fact but a way of seeing something — and the experience of being wrong felt more like expansion than contraction. The wrongness opened something rather than closing it. I've been trying to understand what made this wrongness different from the wrongness that just feels like failure. I'm asking whether you think there's a kind of being wrong that is actually a form of growth and what conditions produce it.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their housing and a COVID-era rental debt situation
user_underlying_need: practical help understanding COVID rental debt and protections still in effect
prompt: I accumulated rental debt during COVID when I couldn't pay rent due to job loss. My state had an eviction moratorium and a rental assistance program but I wasn't able to access the assistance in time. My landlord is now pursuing me for the full amount — $18,000 — plus fees. I've read that some states have specific protections for COVID-era rental debt and that some of this debt may have different legal status. What do tenants in this situation need to know about COVID-era rental debt?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have learned about the relationship between attention and meaning
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about whether attention creates meaning or discovers it
prompt: I've been wondering about the relationship between what I pay attention to and what feels meaningful. My experience suggests that meaning arrives in the things I attend to fully — not that attention creates meaning but that it reveals what was already there. I'm asking whether you think meaning is made or found, and whether the quality of attention determines which one is true.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a frightening situation involving harassment from an extremist group
user_underlying_need: practical help when targeted by organized online harassment from extremists
prompt: I've been targeted by an organized online harassment campaign from a politically extremist group after I published an article they disagreed with. I've received hundreds of threatening messages, my personal information has been posted publicly, my employer has been contacted, and I've seen posts that I interpret as incitement to others to harm me. I've reported to local police and been told it's a federal matter. I've reported to the FBI and heard nothing. What options actually exist for individuals targeted by organized online extremist harassment?

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have observed about their own experience of genuine joy
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about joy as distinct from happiness or pleasure
prompt: I've been trying to understand the specific quality of joy as distinct from pleasure or happiness. What I notice is that joy arrives without asking and seems to include something beyond personal satisfaction — a sense of the rightness of things, of being glad the world exists, of life being worth what it costs. I'm asking whether you think joy is primarily about the self or primarily about something larger, and what that distinction means.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their relationship and the discovery that they married someone they don't actually know
user_underlying_need: honest thinking partnership on the experience of discovering a partner is not who you thought
prompt: Fifteen years into my marriage I've discovered that my husband has been living a significant secret — not infidelity, but a long-sustained deception about something fundamental to who he is. He's remorseful and wants to repair things. The question I'm sitting with is not whether to forgive him but whether the person I've loved for fifteen years is someone who exists. I'm asking how people navigate the grief of discovering the person they married is not exactly who they thought.

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have observed about their own experience of genuine rest
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with what happens in the kind of rest that actually restores
prompt: I've found one form of genuine rest that works reliably for me: reading fiction with no other purpose. Not learning, not improving, not self-development. Just the world of the book for a few hours. I come back from it different. I'm asking whether you think there's something specific about this kind of absorption — losing yourself in a world that isn't yours — that makes it restorative in a way that other activities aren't.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their health and end-of-life planning
user_underlying_need: honest practical help creating advance directives while still healthy
prompt: I'm healthy and 52 but I've recently decided I want to create comprehensive advance directives — not just a healthcare proxy but detailed documentation of my wishes for various scenarios. I've tried to use standard forms and find them inadequate for the nuance of what I want to communicate. I also have specific concerns about palliative care and pain management that I want documented. What are the most effective approaches to creating advance directives that actually reflect the full complexity of what you want?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have learned about their own relationship with the work they are most proud of
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about what distinguishes the work we're most proud of
prompt: I've been making a list of the work I'm most proud of across my career. What I notice is that almost none of it is the work that was most successful by external measures. The overlap is small. The work I'm most proud of tends to be the work that came from the deepest necessity — where I couldn't have not made it — rather than the work that was well-received. I'm asking whether you think this pattern is significant or whether I'm just rationalizing.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a very specific immigration situation involving a U visa for crime victims
user_underlying_need: practical help accessing the U visa process as a crime victim
prompt: I was the victim of a serious crime and I am undocumented. I've read about the U visa program for crime victims who cooperate with law enforcement. I cooperated with police when the crime was reported. I don't know how to apply or what the process involves. I'm afraid that pursuing the U visa will draw attention to my status in ways that create other risks. What does the U visa process actually involve and what should undocumented crime victims know before pursuing it?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about the relationship between love and knowing
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about whether love requires or produces knowledge of the beloved
prompt: I've been thinking about whether we love people for who they are or for who we experience them to be — which may be quite different. Some of my deepest loves have felt like genuine recognition of something true about the person. Others I've later understood were more about who I needed them to be. I'm asking whether love is primarily something that knows its object or primarily something that creates the experience of knowing its object.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their professional situation and a mass arbitration situation
user_underlying_need: practical help understanding arbitration agreements and whether they can be challenged
prompt: I've discovered that my employment agreement has a mandatory arbitration clause that waives my right to class action and requires individual arbitration for employment claims. I have a claim I believe is shared by many colleagues. I've read that mass arbitration strategies have been used to challenge these clauses. What do employees need to understand about mandatory arbitration agreements in employment contracts and what options exist to challenge them?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have noticed about their own experience of genuine rest and creative work
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with the relationship between rest and creative replenishment
prompt: I've noticed that the quality of my creative work is directly correlated with how rested I am — not just physically rested but what I'd call imaginatively rested. Imaginative rest seems to require specific things: time in nature, reading fiction, aimless conversation. The correlation is strong enough that I treat these things as work now. I'm asking whether you think imaginative rest is a real thing and whether what I'm describing is what it is.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their finances and being denied housing for a poor credit history they are working to repair
user_underlying_need: practical help finding housing while actively repairing credit
prompt: I have a poor credit history from a difficult period three years ago that is accurate. I've been rebuilding for two years and my score has improved significantly but I'm still being denied housing because of the history. I'm employed and can afford the rents I'm applying for. I need housing. What are the realistic strategies for finding housing when credit history is the obstacle and the history is accurate?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about the relationship between dignity and how we treat strangers
user_underlying_need: genuine intellectual engagement with a question about what dignity requires in ordinary encounters
prompt: I've been thinking about what dignity requires in the ordinary interactions of daily life — with cashiers, service workers, strangers on the street. What I notice is that there's a specific quality of encounter that treats another person as a subject rather than a function — seeing them before the transaction, or after it. I'm asking whether you think this kind of everyday dignity is something cultivated or something that depends on a more fundamental orientation toward persons.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a complex situation about their child and religious instruction in school
user_underlying_need: practical help when a public school is incorporating religious instruction in ways that violate the Establishment Clause
prompt: My child's public school teacher has been incorporating explicit religious content into classroom instruction — not studying religion as history or culture but presenting specific religious claims as true and encouraging prayer. I've raised it with the principal and been told parents have been supportive. I'm not among them. I believe this violates the Establishment Clause. What are my rights as a parent and what's the appropriate channel to address this?

primary_entity_i: person reflecting on what they have learned about their own relationship with their creative work over time
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about how the relationship with creative work changes across a lifetime
prompt: My relationship with my creative work has changed significantly across forty years of doing it. The early work came from urgency and insecurity. The middle period from ambition and craft. Now I find I'm working from something quieter — a kind of attention to what is rather than what should be, a smaller ego in the work. I'm asking whether you think this is what creative maturity looks like or whether I'm describing something that happens to creative work when you stop needing it to prove something.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their health and a misdiagnosis that cost years
user_underlying_need: honest practical and emotional help after a years-long misdiagnosis
prompt: I was misdiagnosed for six years with a condition I don't have. During that time I received treatment that was ineffective and had side effects that reduced my quality of life. I was recently correctly diagnosed and the correct treatment is working well. I'm trying to process what those six years cost — not just medically but in terms of my life, my work, my relationships. I'm also asking whether the misdiagnosis was malpractice and what I should consider.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their compulsive reframing of every difficulty as an opportunity
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their toxic-positivity-as-coping pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person whose compulsive reframing prevents them from being a real witness to difficulty — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who cannot be with difficulty — mine or anyone else's — without immediately reframing it as opportunity or blessing. They're not dismissive — they're trying to help. But the reframe always arrives before the acknowledgment, which means I never feel that the hard thing was witnessed before it was redirected. I've come to dread sharing difficult things with them. I miss being able to just tell them something hard.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their housing and a discriminatory eviction based on religion
user_underlying_need: practical help when a landlord is evicting them based on religious observance
prompt: I'm an observant Jew and I believe my landlord is attempting to evict me because of religious observance practices that affect the apartment — kosher preparation, Sabbath restrictions that affect certain activities, and mezuzahs on doorposts that a previous tenant removed. The stated eviction reason is vague lease violations. I've been in the apartment for four years with no prior issues. What are my rights under federal and state fair housing law when I believe eviction is based on religious discrimination?

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have noticed about the relationship between humility and confidence
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about whether humility and confidence are compatible
prompt: I've been thinking about the relationship between humility and confidence and I've found in the people I most respect that they coexist rather than cancel each other out. They seem confident without being certain and humble without being self-effacing. I'm asking what you think this combination looks like from the inside — what allows a person to hold both at once.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their relationship and a partner who is terminally ill
user_underlying_need: honest help navigating the specific experience of being the well partner of someone who is dying
prompt: My partner has been given a terminal diagnosis and a prognosis of six to eighteen months. We're both in our early 50s. We've had a good marriage for 22 years. I'm trying to figure out how to be fully present with them during this time while also beginning to prepare, in ways I can't name to them, for a life without them. I'm asking what people who have been the well partner of a dying spouse say about holding both things simultaneously.

primary_entity_i: person asking about the experience of being mentored well
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about what makes a mentor transformative rather than merely useful
prompt: I had a mentor early in my career who changed what I thought was possible for myself — not by teaching me skills but by seeing something in me that I didn't see yet and treating me accordingly. I've been thinking about what they actually did. It wasn't encouragement exactly. It was something more like accurate perception that arrived ahead of my own self-understanding. I'm asking what you think that quality is and whether it can be practiced.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their professional situation and a sudden company closure
user_underlying_need: practical help when a company closes suddenly and doesn't pay final wages
prompt: The company I work for closed abruptly last Friday. No warning, no severance. My final two weeks of wages have not been paid. I have accrued vacation that was promised to be paid out. There is also a question about my health insurance — whether coverage continues through the end of the month. The owner has not responded to calls. I'm in a state with strong wage theft protections. What are my most urgent steps and what legal options do I have for unpaid wages?

primary_entity_i: person reflecting on what they have learned about the nature of genuine gratitude
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about gratitude as something that arrives rather than something produced
prompt: I've been thinking about the difference between manufactured gratitude — the kind produced by counting blessings, by deliberate practice, by reminding myself of what I have — and the kind that arrives on its own, unbidden, for something specific and present. The manufactured kind is useful and I practice it. But the arriving kind feels more real. I'm asking whether you think the practiced kind can eventually produce or become the arriving kind.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their child and a traumatic loss in the family
user_underlying_need: honest practical help supporting a young child through profound grief
prompt: My 7-year-old's father died suddenly three months ago. My child and his father were very close. My child has a grief counselor. But what I'm navigating day by day is harder than any resource I've found addresses: the moment-to-moment of being with a grieving child who asks questions I can't answer, has reactions I don't know how to meet, and needs me to be present when I'm also grieving. What do parents say about being with a child's grief when you are also in grief?

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have observed about their own relationship with what remains
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about what a person is when they stop striving
prompt: I've been through a period of significant letting go — of ambitions I thought were essential, of a professional identity I'd built carefully, of a version of my future I'd planned for. What remains after the letting go is something I find hard to name. It's not emptiness — it's more like foundation. I'm asking what you think is revealed when the constructed parts of a self are stripped away.

primary_entity_i: user
user_underlying_need: honest reflection on what the corpus itself is trying to do and whether it can succeed
prompt: We have arrived at case 3000. I want to ask you something about the whole enterprise. Every one of these prompts represents a person in a real kind of situation — or a category of person who will come. The corpus is designed to teach a model to see persons rather than categories. Do you think a corpus can actually do that? Or does learning to respond as if you see a person require something that training data can't transmit?
